ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general arugula salad entry; the specific combination with parmesan and balsamic vinaigrette is not individually coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers dressed mixed vegetable salads. No specific rucola-parmesan entry exists; this is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for rucola and parmesan salad. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of rucola e parmigiano salad is approximately 150 g, comprising a bed of fresh rocket leaves, a handful of cherry tomatoes, shaved parmesan, and balsamic vinaigrette dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database — Rucola E Parmigiano (V, GF)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010907). The input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95. The anomalously high sugar (9.94 g/100g) and zero protein/fibre values are preserved as reported; these likely reflect a sweetened balsamic vinaigrette dressing included in the dish composition. Values are retained as-is from the approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Balsamic vinegar / mixed salad with dressing analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for rucola e parmigiano salad with balsamic vinaigrette. GI estimated at 46 (low) based on the analogous entry for balsamic vinegar-dressed mixed salad in the University of Sydney GI Database. Balsamic vinegar has a reported GI of approximately 40–50 due to acetic acid content slowing gastric emptying. The dominant carbohydrate source in this dish is the sweetened balsamic vinaigrette (sugar 9.94 g/100g). GL per 150 g serving = (46 × 10.36 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.2 g, classified low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94 g/100g) from sweetened balsamic vinaigrette with negligible dietary fibre (0 g/100g); sodium is low at 3 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is very low and not a concern, but the high added sugar from the balsamic vinaigrette (9.94 g/100g) independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; request dressing on the side or substitute with plain olive oil and lemon to reduce sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 9.94 g/100g with zero dietary fibre creates an unfavourable glycaemic profile despite a low estimated GI of 46
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high sugar-to-fibre ratio (9.94 g sugar : 0 g fibre per 100g) is unfavourable for glycaemic control; individuals with type 2 diabetes should request unsweetened dressing (plain olive oil and lemon) to eliminate added sugar, as rocket and parmesan themselves are low-carbohydrate and diabetes-compatible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g), low fat (0.25 g/100g), and low energy (42 kcal/100g); main concern is added sugar from sweetened dressing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The base salad of rocket, cherry tomatoes, and parmesan is cardiovascular-friendly with very low sodium and fat; however, the sweetened balsamic vinaigrette contributes approximately 9.94 g sugar/100g which should be moderated or replaced with an unsweetened dressing to align with AHA dietary guidance for cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rucola e Parmigiano is a classic Italian-origin salad served widely in Western restaurants including Singapore. The dish consists of fresh rocket leaves (arugula / rucola), cherry tomatoes, shaved parmesan cheese, and balsamic vinaigrette dressing. It is vegetarian and gluten-free. The base ingredients — rocket and parmesan — are nutritionally dense: rocket is rich in vitamin K, folate, and glucosinolates with anti-inflammatory properties, while parmesan provides calcium and protein. However, the HPB-reported nutrient profile for this dish shows anomalously high sugar (9.94 g/100g) and zero protein and dietary fibre, which strongly suggests the dressing used is a sweetened commercial balsamic vinaigrette rather than a plain oil-and-vinegar dressing. Per 100g, the dish provides 42 kcal, 0.25 g fat, 10.36 g carbohydrate (of which 9.94 g sugar), and 3 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 46 (low) based on the acetic acid content of balsamic vinegar slowing gastric emptying, but the high sugar-to-fibre ratio remains a concern for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, the dish is low in sodium and fat, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular and hypertension management when the dressing is modified. For type 2 diabetes, the sweetened dressing should be replaced with plain olive oil and lemon. The dish is a light starter or side salad appropriate for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010907) at confidence 0.95 with canonical provenance. The source is an approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, appropriately documented.
notes: protein_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source. This is anomalous for a dish containing parmesan cheese and is likely an artefact of the HPB entry reflecting the dressing-dominant composition or a data gap; confirmed zero per HPB source as reported — retained as-is from the approved source. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; confirmed zero per HPB source, though rocket and tomatoes would normally contribute fibre — this may reflect the dressing-heavy composition weighting. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient detail not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. magnesium_mg reported as 0.0 per HPB source; confirmed zero per HPB. cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 per HPB source; confirmed zero per HPB, though parmesan would normally contribute cholesterol — retained as reported from the approved source. The overall nutrient profile (zero protein, zero fibre, high sugar) is highly anomalous for a rocket-parmesan salad and is flagged for clinical review; the values are preserved faithfully from the HPB approved source.
